questions.scenario: A single Nginx process hosts `app.example.com` and `admin.example.com`. A request for `https://app.example.com/api/orders` reaches the machine. Which matching sequence determines its handler?

options.text: Nginx selects the shortest location first, then asks DNS which server block owns the app process
Nginx selects a server block from the listener and requested hostname, then selects the most appropriate location for `/api/orders` inside that site
Nginx sends every request to the default app server before reading the Host header or request path
The firewall selects the Nginx location block from the destination port and rewrites the HTTP path

questions.explanation: Nginx first chooses the virtual server using listener and hostname information, then chooses a location based on the request URI. DNS and firewalls get traffic to Nginx but do not select its internal location blocks. The [Nginx request processing guide](https://nginx.org/en/docs/http/request_processing.html) explains server and location selection.

questions.scenario: A React site serves `/` and `/assets/app.js`, but refreshing `/dashboard/settings` returns 404. The file `/var/www/app/index.html` exists and is readable. Which location block best preserves real assets and supports client-side routes?

options.text: `location / { try_files /index.html; }`, which replaces every asset request with the SPA shell
`location / { proxy_pass http://127.0.0.1:3000; }`, because static files must always pass through the app
`location / { try_files $host $uri =403; }`, which maps the requested hostname to a file path
`location / { try_files $uri $uri/ /index.html; }`, which serves real paths first and falls back to the SPA shell

questions.explanation: The desired order is exact file, directory, then `index.html` fallback. Serving the shell unconditionally can hide missing assets, while proxying all static traffic wastes the web server separation. Hostnames are not filesystem paths. The [Nginx `try_files` documentation](https://nginx.org/en/docs/http/ngx_http_core_module.html#try_files) defines this lookup behavior.

options.text: Use `proxy_pass http://127.0.0.1:3000/;` so Nginx replaces the matched `/api/` prefix with `/`
Move the API into `try_files` so missing upstream routes fall back to `index.html`
Change the public path to `/users` because Nginx cannot rewrite location prefixes
Add `index users` because the index directive controls the path sent to a proxied app

questions.explanation: With a URI part in `proxy_pass`, here the trailing `/`, Nginx replaces the matching location prefix. `/api/users` therefore becomes `/users`. A SPA fallback would hide API errors, and `index` applies to directory file serving. The [official `proxy_pass` reference](https://nginx.org/en/docs/http/ngx_http_proxy_module.html#proxy_pass) documents URI replacement.

questions.scenario: After moving an app behind Nginx, every audit log shows client IP `127.0.0.1`, rate limiting groups all users together, and generated redirects point to `http://127.0.0.1:3000`. Which correction best addresses all three symptoms?

options.text: Expose the app port publicly so clients bypass Nginx and provide their own forwarding headers
Lower the DNS TTL so the app sees the public hostname on its next resolver lookup
Forward Host, client IP chain, and original scheme headers, then configure the app to trust only the intended proxy path
Replace `proxy_pass` with a static root because forwarded context applies only to file responses

questions.explanation: `Host`, `X-Real-IP`, `X-Forwarded-For`, and `X-Forwarded-Proto` preserve the public request context. The app must trust the controlled proxy path because direct clients can spoof these headers if the backend is exposed. DNS does not repair HTTP context, and bypassing Nginx weakens the boundary. The [Nginx proxy header documentation](https://nginx.org/en/docs/http/ngx_http_proxy_module.html#proxy_set_header) covers forwarded fields.

questions.scenario: A new HTTPS server block points `ssl_certificate` at `cert.pem`, which contains only the leaf certificate. Some browsers work, but clean webhook clients report `unable to get local issuer certificate`. Which deployment change is appropriate?

options.text: Point `ssl_certificate` at the full chain file, protect the matching private key, test with `nginx -t`, reload, and verify the public certificate
Put the private key inside the certificate file sent to clients so they can validate ownership directly
Remove SNI support so every hostname receives the same leaf certificate and cached intermediates
Proxy HTTPS to the app before Nginx reads the certificate because upstream status determines trust

questions.explanation: Clients need the leaf and required intermediates, while the private key must remain secret on the server. The config should be tested before reload and verified from outside. SNI chooses the right virtual host, and upstream HTTP status occurs after public TLS succeeds. The [Nginx HTTPS guide](https://nginx.org/en/docs/http/configuring_https_servers.html) covers certificate chains and SNI.

questions.scenario: A retailer scales its checkout application from one process to three behind an Nginx round-robin upstream. All three health checks pass, but users lose authenticated sessions when consecutive requests reach different processes. Sessions must survive process restarts and future scaling events. Which design change best meets that requirement?

options.text: Use IP-based affinity so most users return to one process, while leaving session data only in that process memory
Store sessions in shared state such as Redis, a database, or signed cookies so any healthy backend can serve the next request
Reduce the upstream to one process so in-memory sessions work, accepting that scaling and process failure remain unavailable
Configure longer passive health-failure timers so a restarted process recovers its previous in-memory sessions

questions.explanation: Shared or client-contained session state lets any healthy process serve the next request and preserves the scaling requirement. Affinity can reduce cross-process movement but does not survive process loss. Returning to one process abandons resilience and scale, while health timers cannot restore volatile memory. [The Nginx load balancing guide](https://docs.nginx.com/nginx/admin-guide/load-balancer/http-load-balancer/) explains upstream distribution.

questions.scenario:
The public endpoint returns `502 Bad Gateway`. Nginx logs:

```text
connect() failed (111: Connection refused) while connecting to upstream,
upstream: "http://127.0.0.1:3000/api/health"
```

`nginx -t` succeeds and Nginx owns ports `80` and `443`. Which next check is most direct?

options.text: Query the public DNS nameservers because 502 proves the client never reached Nginx
Raise `proxy_read_timeout` because a refused connection needs more time to establish
Inspect the app process and test `127.0.0.1:3000` locally because Nginx could not connect to the configured upstream listener
Change the SPA fallback because `index.html` controls whether the app process binds its port

questions.explanation: The response and log prove the request reached Nginx, which then received a refusal from the configured backend address. Verify that the app is running and listening where `proxy_pass` expects. A timeout increase cannot fix an immediate refusal, and DNS or SPA routing is not the failed handoff. The [Nginx reverse proxy guide](https://docs.nginx.com/nginx/admin-guide/web-server/reverse-proxy/) describes upstream communication.

questions.scenario: A release changes Nginx routing, adds a WebSocket endpoint, and keeps a server-sent event stream. Which review comments are correct? Select all that apply.

options.text: Restart Nginx before testing the config so the new process can report syntax errors
Run `nginx -t` before reload and verify both a local upstream URL and the public hostname afterward
Proxy WebSockets with HTTP/1.1 plus the Upgrade and Connection headers expected by the upstream
Enable response buffering for the event stream so each event reaches the browser only after the connection closes
Disable proxy buffering and caching for the live event route when the application expects chunks to reach clients promptly

questions.explanation: A safe rollout validates syntax before reload and tests both sides of the proxy. WebSocket upgrades need the appropriate HTTP/1.1 upgrade headers, while live event streams often need buffering and caching disabled. Restarting first risks downtime, and buffering a stream until close defeats the live behavior. The [Nginx proxy module reference](https://nginx.org/en/docs/http/ngx_http_proxy_module.html) covers upgrade headers and buffering controls.
